summary: I was born in Virginia, Illinois on April 1, 1940. It's a very small rural town in south central Illinois with less than a thousand people, surrounded by farmland. Although I only lived there for about two years and don't remember much, I have visited since then to see the place where I was born.

In 1941, when I was about a year old, my sister Marian and I were taken to live at The Baby Fold, an orphanage in Normal, Illinois. I stayed there with my sister for about six or seven months in 1942 before being adopted. The Baby Fold is still operating in Normal, and I even gave a talk at their Founder's Day banquet recently, which was an emotional experience given how much adoption has shaped my life.

Normal, Illinois is where The Baby Fold orphanage is located, and where my sister Marian and I lived for a short time in 1941 and 1942. It's not far from Clinton, Illinois, where we were later adopted. I have a connection to Normal through The Baby Fold and the early part of my life before adoption.

Marian is my sister, and we were taken to The Baby Fold together in 1941 when we were very young. We were not separated during that time or after adoption. Marian and I were adopted as siblings by James and Bernice McKibben in 1942, and we grew up together in Clinton, Illinois.

James Dewey McKibben, along with his wife Bernice, came to The Baby Fold in October 1942 looking to adopt a child. Although initially hesitant about adopting two children, they decided to adopt both Marian and me. James was the superintendent of the school district in Clinton, Illinois, where we lived after the adoption was finalized in 1943.

Bernice Brown McKibben was my adoptive mother who, together with her husband James, adopted my sister Marian and me in 1942. She was an English and Latin teacher but stayed home to care for us after the adoption. We lived with her and James in Clinton, Illinois until 1948.

Clinton, Illinois is where my adoptive parents, James and Bernice McKibben, lived when they adopted my sister and me in late 1942. I grew up there until 1948, finishing second grade before we moved. My father was the superintendent of the school district there, and my mother cared for us at home.

The adoption of my sister Marian and me was finalized in 1943 at the McLean County Court. This legal step made James and Bernice McKibben our official parents after we had been placed in their home in Clinton, Illinois. The court played an important role in formalizing our new family.

After living in Clinton until 1948, my family moved to Oneida, Illinois, which is about fifty miles north of Peoria. I lived there through my childhood and graduated from high school in 1958. Oneida was my home during my school years after the move from Clinton.

Peoria, Illinois is a city located about fifty miles south of Oneida, where I lived after 1948. While I didn't live in Peoria itself, it was a nearby reference point for where my family settled in northwestern Illinois. Peoria helped me describe the location of Oneida during my childhood.
